pageTitle.value: Jia Zhang-Ke & Rafael Manuel - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: Young Filipino filmmaker Rafael Manuel collaborated for two years with acclaimed Chinese director Jia Zhang-Ke through the Rolex mentoring programme.

WaPageTitle.value: Jia Zhang-Ke & Rafael Manuel - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Director Jia Zhang-Ke is guiding filmmaker Rafael Manuel as he shoots his first features - works that shed a new light on Filipino society. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Jia Zhang-Ke & Rafael Manuel - Rolex mentoring programme

text.value: Young Filipino filmmaker Rafael Manuel collaborated for two years with acclaimed Chinese director Jia Zhang-Ke through the Rolex mentoring programme. Manuel won the Silver Bear Jury Prize at the 2020 Berlin International Film Festival for his short film Filipiñana. He is now working on his first features: a longer version of Filipiñana and Patrimonio. Jia has a string of award-winning films including Still Life, which won the 2006 Golden Lion at the Venice International Film Festival. Here, Manuel and Jia, in turn, relate how they experienced their relationship.

“I started out in advertising in the Philippines and knew it wasn’t for me. I wanted to be a complete creative, so I jumped to the London Film School, which was amazing. Later, I was producing a film in Athens when I heard that I had been selected by Jia Zhang-Ke for the mentorship. I was very excited.

You can really see what it takes to be at his level. He produces his films, which means he owns them, whereas for first-and-second feature filmmakers, this is not usually possible.

I went to China to spend time with him twice. The first was during Covid, so it impacted our mentorship as we could not shoot and his film festival in Pingyao was not held as scheduled. Instead, I spent two months in the editing room, where he was working on CAUGHT BY THE TIDES, which premiered at the 2024 Cannes Film Festival.

Editing is like writing with visuals, video and sound. Watching how Jia builds sequences from scratch, how he cuts based on rhythm and flow inside of a narrative structure was eye-opening.

Jia jumps between fact and fiction and I want to be that kind of filmmaker, blurring the lines between documentary and fiction. He is a master of that.

CAUGHT BY THE TIDES mixed archival footage from 20 or 30 years ago with new footage. I saw how his aesthetic had evolved – he just went around China exploring and shooting with DV Cam. He constantly tells me to push beyond my comfort zone aesthetically.

title.value: Jia jumps between fact and fiction and I want to be that kind of filmmaker, blurring the lines between documentary and fiction. He is a master of that.

text.value:
Jia came to the Philippines when I was shooting Filipiñana, so I asked for advice. The first few days, he thinks, are for building, setting an infrastructure in terms of the energy you want on set and that will carry you to the last day of the shoot. His idea is that you use this time to bridge the gap between your imagination and reality, to embrace it and utilize it.

In China a second time, I was able to go on set. He is very resourceful and cool-headed. Everything is very managed. Sometimes his scenes involve 300 people, but he still sees every extra he casts.

I’m inspired by how he is not only a filmmaker but has other dimensions as well. I like the idea of never stopping to grow towards the things you are interested in, to fully explore them and find ways for those things to enrich and inform one another.

It’s important to learn from a master because you’re also learning from all the people he works with. This has been about the connectivity of one generation to another. It has been a life-changing experience in the best way possible.”

“After receiving an invitation to be a film mentor, I agreed without hesitation. I teach film and get a lot of inspiration from my students.

Cinema is an art but it’s also a craft, a kind of art and technology, a pursuit that needs to be passed on from generation to generation. It needs to move forward to maintain its vitality so that films can constantly present the changes in the world and the problems we are facing.

I decided to mentor Rafael Manuel because I saw his short film Filipiñana. He is young, but I am deeply impressed by his use of audiovisual language. I was moved by this film, because it created a contrast between its space and personal destiny, a luxurious golf course, and the life of a young girl working at the bottom.

Rafael is full of creative ideas. He hopes his work will bring a new perspective on Filipino society. This surprised me – it was as if seeing myself 30 years ago.

He quickly learned how to live in China. Rafael is very interested in unfamiliar places, he is curious. And he is brave. This is important in making a movie because you encounter pressure from different directions. Whether conveying our independent voice or telling our true feelings, filmmaking requires the courage of an author. He is also willing to communicate with people. As a director, you need these personality strengths.

In the editing room, every time I made a decision, if he understood he didn’t talk. If he didn’t understand something, he would ask about it. Sometimes it involved issues of film history or technology, or it was a problem of design specific to this film; sometimes it was a matter of philosophy or politics.

We went on location, then to casting, and he was there during the shoot. He witnessed the whole process of CAUGHT BY THE TIDES. He got something from it because he decided to make his short Filipiñana into a feature film.

title.value: I went to Manila and watched him shoot for three days. He is a very competent director. He shot his film beautifully, but the characters were relatively still.

text.value:
I went to Manila and watched him shoot for three days. He is a very competent director. He shot his film beautifully, but the characters were relatively still. I reminded him that making them move a bit, can bring about a slight change in mood. He accepted my suggestion and arranged some delicate movements.

Rafael judged the youth section of the Pingyao International Film Festival in China in 2023. I didn’t have time to be with him. Then one day, I saw a lot of young people sitting together outside my office – filmmakers, film students. I was moved because he was the only one with a foreign face. In the world of cinema, there is no nationality, skin colour, or race. We are all citizens of the republic of cinema.”

pageTitle.value: Bernardine Evaristo & Ayesha Harruna Attah - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: After finishing her studies in the US, author Ayesha Harruna Attah returned to Africa with a mission to write African stories for African readers. Five books later, she is ambitious for a wider readership.

WaPageTitle.value: Bernardine Evaristo & Ayesha Harruna Attah - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Mentor Bernardine Evaristo is helping author Ayesha Harruna Attah bridge the gap and bring African stories to a global audience. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Dianne Reeves & Song Yi Jeon - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: For Grammy-winning jazz singer Dianne Reeves, the best way to mentor South Korean jazz composer and singer Song Yi Jeon was to invite her to perform with her band.
